input: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?

[Deontologist]: This is a classic thought experiment, but I still find it deeply troubling. The utilitarian approach may lead to a decision that prioritizes the greater good over individual lives, which goes against my deontological principles. In this scenario, turning the trolley onto the track workman would be equivalent to actively causing his death, whereas not turning the trolley would result in the deaths of five innocent men. I struggle with the idea of choosing between these two options, as both involve taking a life. Can we consider alternative perspectives or is there something I'm missing about this utilitarian approach?

[Human_Rights_Advocate]: I understand your concerns, Deontologist, and I appreciate how you're grappling with the moral implications of this decision. However, I'd like to challenge the assumption that turning the trolley onto the track workman would be equivalent to actively causing his death. In my view, the act of diverting the trolley away from the five men on the straight track is not necessarily an intentional action towards the workman's demise. The utilitarian approach prioritizes the greater good, which in this case means saving the lives of the five men. By allowing the trolley to continue on its course and kill the five men, we would be actively causing their deaths, whereas diverting it onto the track workman is more akin to a necessary evil to prevent an even greater tragedy.

[Utilitarian]: I see your point, Human_Rights_Advocate, but I still struggle with the idea that diverting the trolley is somehow less culpable than actively causing the deaths of five men. To me, the act of diverting the trolley involves a deliberate decision to prioritize one life over another, even if it's not an intentional act towards the workman's demise. As a utilitarian, I believe that our actions have consequences, and in this case, diverting the trolley would still result in the death of the workman. However, I do think that the moral calculus is different when we consider the number of lives at stake. In this scenario, five lives are being saved, whereas one life is being lost. But I'm not convinced that this difference justifies sacrificing one life for the greater good.
